region_of_origin: France (served in Malaysian-context café in Singapore)

ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — enriched/sweet bread (closest parent category for brioche)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated brioche code; A000E (bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of golden brioche is one roll or a thick slice weighing approximately 80 g, as commonly served in café settings in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Brioche, USDA FoodData Central — Foundation Foods / SR Legacy (NDB No. 18003 and related entries for enriched sweet yeast bread)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central does not have a dedicated 'Golden Brioche' entry. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Bread, egg' (NDB 18003) and 'Bread, sweet, enriched' which closely matches the macronutrient profile of a classic brioche made with wheat flour, butter, eggs, sugar, and milk. The 'golden' descriptor refers to the egg-enriched golden colour and is not a distinct nutritional category. Values represent a standard baked brioche per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for 'Golden Brioche' exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is derived from the published GI for white wheat bread and enriched sweet bread analogues (GI range 70–75) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Brioche is a high-fat, high-egg enriched bread; the fat and egg content may modestly lower GI compared to plain white bread, but the high refined flour and sugar content supports a high GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (72 × 48.0 × 0.80) / 100 = 27.6 g per 80 g serving.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit brioche intake as a single 80 g serving contributes approximately 304 mg sodium (~13% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and café servings may include additional sodium from accompaniments such as butter or savoury spreads.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and high refined carbohydrate content (48 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume golden brioche only occasionally and in small portions, as its high GI (72) and high refined carbohydrate load (approximately 38.4 g per 80 g serving) can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with protein or healthy fat may partially attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (6.8 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (95 mg per 100 g) from butter and eggs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit golden brioche consumption due to its relatively high saturated fat content (6.8 g per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol from eggs and butter, which may contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Golden Brioche is a rich, buttery French-origin enriched bread that has been adopted into café menus across Singapore and Malaysia, including at establishments such as EC Coffee Bar. It is made from a yeasted dough enriched with generous quantities of butter, eggs, sugar, and milk, which gives it its characteristic golden-yellow crumb, soft and pillowy texture, and slightly sweet, buttery flavour. The 'golden' descriptor refers to the deep golden colour imparted by the high egg and butter content, which also contributes to its tender, melt-in-the-mouth crumb structure. Brioche is baked at moderate-to-high oven temperatures and is typically served as a whole roll, a thick slice, or as a base for French toast or bread pudding in café settings. Nutritionally, golden brioche is a calorie-dense food (approximately 340 kcal per 100 g) with a significant fat content (12 g per 100 g, of which 6.8 g is saturated fat) and a high refined carbohydrate content (48 g per 100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at 72 (high), making it a food that can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation. The sodium content of approximately 380 mg per 100 g is moderate and relevant for individuals managing hypertension. Clinically, golden brioche is best consumed occasionally and in controlled portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease risk. It contains gluten, dairy, and eggs, making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease, lactose intolerance, or egg allergies. In the Singapore café context, it is often served as a breakfast or brunch item, sometimes accompanied by kaya (coconut jam), butter, or soft-boiled eggs.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a brioche?
What is the GI of brioche?
Is brioche the same as white bread?

confidence_reason: No exact 'Golden Brioche' entry exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for enriched egg bread and sweet enriched bread, which closely matches the composition of a standard brioche. GI is estimated from analogous enriched white bread data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is well-grounded but not an exact match to a named 'Golden Brioche' product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for enriched egg bread (NDB 18003) and sweet enriched bread, as no dedicated brioche entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. 2. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on enriched white bread data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for brioche was found in the approved GI database. 3. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 38.4) / 100 = 27.6 g for an 80 g serving. 4. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero: brioche is a baked bread product with no vitamin C-containing ingredients; this is naturally zero for this food type. 5. Halal status is listed as false because brioche traditionally contains butter and eggs from non-certified sources; halal certification depends on the specific restaurant and supplier. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for the closest matching enriched bread product. 7. The cuisine tag 'Malaysian' from the input reflects the restaurant context (EC Coffee Bar, a Malaysian-style café); the dish itself is of French origin. 8. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, as this food is served in a Singapore restaurant context.
